era_context: The late 14th century in Europe was marked by the aftermath of the Black Death (1347–1353), which had killed 30–60% of Europe's population. The Hundred Years' War between England and France continued with intermittent campaigns. The Ottoman Empire was expanding in the Balkans and Anatolia. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty (founded 1368) was consolidating control over China after overthrowing the Mongol Yuan. The Papacy remained in Avignon, and the Western Church faced growing institutional criticism.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records from this period)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; local chronicles less accessible)
Americas (no European contact; indigenous records limited)

events.title: Treaty of Bruges (English-Burgundian Alliance)
events.description: In 1373, English forces under John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster, negotiated or reinforced alliances with Burgundian powers as part of the broader Hundred Years' War strategy. The English sought to isolate France by securing Burgundian support and preventing French expansion in the Low Countries. These diplomatic maneuvers were part of the complex web of alliances that characterized the later phases of the Hundred Years' War. The exact nature and date of specific treaties in 1373 are somewhat unclear in sources, but the general pattern of English-Burgundian cooperation is well-attested.
key_figures: John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster (English commander)
Philip II, Duke of Burgundy (Burgundian ruler)
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of English-Burgundian alliance-building in the early 1370s is well-documented, but the precise date and nature of specific treaties in 1373 are less clear. Dating is approximate within ±2 years.
cross_references: Part of Hundred Years' War diplomacy (1337–1453)

events.title: Ming Dynasty Administrative Reforms
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued administrative and military reforms in 1373 as part of the broader consolidation of Ming power. The Ming court was reorganizing the examination system, restructuring the military (particularly the establishment of the Guard system), and eliminating remaining Yuan loyalists and rival power centers. These reforms were foundational to the Ming Dynasty's long-term stability and centralized governance. The period saw increasing emphasis on Confucian orthodoxy and imperial authority.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Ming administrative reform is well-documented, but specific events of 1373 are not individually highlighted in most sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The year 1373 is not marked by any major historical turning points or widely recognized events. Most historical narratives skip over this year or subsume it into broader patterns of the early 1370s. The significance of specific events attributed to 1373 (such as English-Burgundian diplomatic maneuvers) is often overstated or conflated with events from adjacent years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1373 is sparse and fragmented. Western European events are documented in chronicles and administrative records, but specific events of 1373 are often not clearly distinguished from those of adjacent years. East Asian events (Ming consolidation) are well-documented in Chinese sources but lack specific annual detail for 1373. The year represents a relatively quiet period in global history, with no major turning points or widely recognized events.

graph_edges.note: English-Burgundian diplomatic maneuvers continued alongside the broader Hundred Years' War conflict
Ming administrative reforms continued as part of the broader consolidation process
